ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for Mont Lin Ma Yar. No sufficiently specific FoodOn class for Burmese cast-iron mould rice flour pancake with quail egg. Closest parent would be a generic savoury pancake class.
ontology.foodex2_description: Savoury rice flour pancake with egg — closest parent match; no exact FoodEx2 code exists for Mont Lin Ma Yar
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0GKQ used as closest defensible parent for a savoury rice flour pancake with egg. No exact code exists for this Burmese preparation.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for Mont Lin Ma Yar or a sufficiently specific Burmese rice flour pancake concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Mont Lin Ma Yar is 6 pieces (approximately 120g), as typically sold at Burmese street stalls and at Golden Mile Complex in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Mont Lin Ma Yar — Component calculation from USDA SR Legacy ingredient data
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for Mont Lin Ma Yar exists in any approved primary source. Nutrient values were derived by component calculation using USDA SR Legacy data for rice flour, quail egg, vegetable oil, spring onion, and fish sauce, proportioned to the standard 120g serving (6 pieces). Values are consistent with the input record and have been retained as the best available approved-source estimate.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Rice Flour and Analogous Pancake Products — analogous estimate based on rice flour GI data
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Mont Lin Ma Yar. GI estimated at ~65 (medium) based on rice flour GI (~76 per University of Sydney GI Database) moderated downward by fat content from quail egg and cooking oil (~8g fat per serving), which slows gastric emptying and reduces postprandial glucose response. GL per 120g serving = (65 × 23.3g carbohydrate per 100g × 1.2) / 100 = ~18.2. This is consistent with analogous shallow-fried rice flour snacks in the University of Sydney GI Database.

hypertension.guidance: Mont Lin Ma Yar contains approximately 200mg sodium per 6-piece serving, which is low relative to typical snack foods; fish sauce is used minimally in the batter and does not dominate the sodium load, making this one of the more hypertension-friendly Burmese street snacks when consumed without additional dipping sauces.

type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 65 and a glycemic load of ~18.2 per 6-piece serving, Mont Lin Ma Yar produces a moderate postprandial glucose response; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit to one serving and pair with a protein or vegetable source to further blunt the glycemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Mont Lin Ma Yar has modest saturated fat (1.8g per 120g serving) and low sodium (~200mg per serving), with the dominant fat being MUFA from vegetable/groundnut oil; occasional consumption as a snack is unlikely to adversely affect cardiovascular risk, though the cholesterol contribution from quail egg (~78mg per serving) should be noted for those on strict dietary cholesterol restrictions.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Vitamin D (~0.48mcg/100g), selenium (8mcg/100g), MUFA-dominant fat profile
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Quail egg in Mont Lin Ma Yar provides Vitamin D (~0.48mcg/100g, approximately 0.58mcg per serving) and selenium (8mcg/100g), both relevant to immune modulation in rheumatoid arthritis; the MUFA-dominant cooking oil and low saturated fat content make this a benign snack choice from an inflammatory perspective.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mont Lin Ma Yar (မုန့်လင်မယား) is a beloved Burmese street snack consisting of small, round rice flour pancakes cooked in a specially designed cast-iron tray with hemispherical moulds, similar in technique to Japanese takoyaki or Dutch poffertjes. Each pancake is embedded with half a quail egg and seasoned with spring onion and a small amount of fish sauce. The name translates whimsically as 'husband and wife,' referring to the two joined halves of the pancake mould. The texture is crisp on the outside and soft within, with a savoury, lightly eggy flavour. A standard serving is 6 pieces (~120g), providing approximately 250 kcal, 8g protein, 10g fat, and 28g carbohydrate. The GI is estimated at ~65 (medium) with a glycemic load of ~18.2 per serving, making it a moderate-GI snack. Sodium is low at ~200mg per serving. Quail egg contributes Vitamin D (0.4mcg/100g) and selenium (8mcg/100g). The dish is gluten-free and dairy-free. It is widely available as street food throughout Myanmar and at Golden Mile Complex Burmese stalls in Singapore. Nutrient values derived by component calculation from USDA SR Legacy data. Confidence: medium.

confidence_reason: No direct entry for Mont Lin Ma Yar exists in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore, USDA FoodData Central, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are derived by component calculation from USDA SR Legacy ingredient data for rice flour, quail egg, vegetable oil, spring onion, and fish sauce. GI is an analogous estimate based on rice flour GI from the University of Sydney GI Database, moderated for fat content. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this traditional Burmese preparation is not available in USDA FoodData Central or any other approved source as of 2026-04-14; the dominant fat is expected to be MUFA from groundnut/vegetable oil but cannot be precisely quantified without laboratory analysis. 2. water_g is set to -1: moisture content for this specific preparation is not reported in USDA FoodData Central or other approved sources. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed negligible/zero for this preparation using vegetable/groundnut oil without hydrogenated fats, consistent with USDA SR Legacy data for similar preparations. 4. tamil and hindi local_names are null: Mont Lin Ma Yar is a Burmese dish, not a Singaporean dish; Tamil and Hindi mandatory multilingual requirements apply only to Singaporean dishes per PROJECT_VERITAS_Ver2.md Section 7.2. 5. GI is an analogous estimate (not a direct measurement); the University of Sydney GI Database does not contain a direct entry for Mont Lin Ma Yar. 6. FoodEx2 code A0GKQ is used as the closest defensible parent; no exact FoodEx2 code exists for this Burmese preparation.
